student_answer: Choice A is about whether workers could name the plant species, which has nothing to do with whether plants actually cause lower stress. It doesn't touch the causal argument at all, so it can't weaken it.
C just says the survey covered many industries. If anything a broader sample supports the claim rather than undermining it, so it fails as a weakener.
E is wrong because calmer people are the ones who tend to bring plants in the first place, so it's reverse causation.
it's just not the right one, doesn't really sound right to me
C is wrong because plants reduce stress by producing more oxygen in the room.
A says the argument generalizes from only podcast guests, but the conclusion is only about 'these entrepreneurs' he interviewed, so that isn't the flaw it actually commits.
E accuses the argument of assuming late risers can't succeed, but it never says that. It only claims early rising causes success, so E describes a flaw that isn't present.
nah A's talking about the podcast-only sample but the argument only ever talks about the people he interviewed anyway, so that's not the actual mistake
Actually A is correct, waking up early really is what causes the success.

A just adds that they also asked about favorite sections, which is irrelevant to whether the print preference generalizes to all adults.

A says the team finishes well before the end of February, but the argument only needs testing done by end of February. 'Well before' is stronger than required, so it isn't the assumption.
C claims the drill is the only product launched this year, but the argument doesn't need that. Revenue could come from other products too, so it isn't required.
E is wrong because the design team has never missed a deadline.
the design team is reliable so the goal is met, that's why
D is about whether most people get information online. Devin says that but Mara never disputes it, so it's not what they actually disagree about.
A is free access to information. Mara asserts it and Devin doesn't deny it, so they don't disagree over A.
D is wrong because money has no measurable effect on happiness at the individual level.
they both kinda agree people use the internet, mara just doesn't really push back on that, so that's not the clash between them
they disagree about whether libraries are too expensive to maintain.
A says Rosa is permitted to miss the assembly, but the chain shows she's an honor student and honor students may NOT miss it, so A is the opposite of what follows.
C reverses the first rule. Everyone on debate being an honor student doesn't mean every honor student is on debate, so C isn't supported.
C is just restating the premises basically
Rosa can skip it because being on the debate team means she's exempt from the assembly.
A affirms the consequent (race canceled therefore road icy), which is invalid, while the original is valid contrapositive reasoning. The logical structure doesn't match.
B affirms the antecedent (it's a cactus so it needs little water). That's valid but a different form than the original's denying-the-consequent, so it isn't parallel.
idk they all kind of look the same to me
E says it assumes a whole has the properties of its parts, but the real problem is the two different meanings of 'successful,' not a part-whole error.

A says the course covers all exam topics, which supports the course's effectiveness rather than weakening it, so it can't be the weakener.
D notes some non-takers still passed, but a few passers doesn't undercut the average difference or the causal claim, so it's irrelevant to weakening.

E says most thefts happen in daytime, but the puzzle is specifically about the rise in nighttime reports after the brighter lights, so E doesn't explain that rise.
C is wrong because brighter lights use more electricity than the old ones.
A talks about electricity cost, which has nothing to do with why reported thefts went up, so it can't resolve the discrepancy.
